1	package upstream
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"fmt"
6		"io"
7		"log/slog"
8		"net"
9		"net/url"
10		"strings"
11		"time"
12	
13		"github.com/AdguardTeam/dnsproxy/internal/bootstrap"
14		"github.com/AdguardTeam/golibs/errors"
15		"github.com/AdguardTeam/golibs/logutil/slogutil"
16		"github.com/miekg/dns"
17	)
18	
19	// network is the semantic type alias of the network to pass to dialing
20	// functions.  It's either [networkUDP] or [networkTCP].  It may also be used as
21	// URL scheme for plain upstreams.
22	type network = string
23	
24	const (
25		// networkUDP is the UDP network.
26		networkUDP network = "udp"
27	
28		// networkTCP is the TCP network.
29		networkTCP network = "tcp"
30	)
31	
32	// plainDNS implements the [Upstream] interface for the regular DNS protocol.
33	type plainDNS struct {
34		// addr is the DNS server URL.  Scheme is always "udp" or "tcp".
35		addr *url.URL
36	
37		// logger is used for exchange logging.  It is never nil.
38		logger *slog.Logger
39	
40		// getDialer either returns an initialized dial handler or creates a new
41		// one.
42		getDialer DialerInitializer
43	
44		// net is the network of the connections.
45		net network
46	
47		// timeout is the timeout for DNS requests.
48		timeout time.Duration
49	}
50	
51	// newPlain returns the plain DNS Upstream.  addr.Scheme should be either "udp"
52	// or "tcp".
53	func newPlain(addr *url.URL, opts *Options) (u *plainDNS, err error) {
54		switch addr.Scheme {
55		case networkUDP, networkTCP:
56			// Go on.
57		default:
58			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported url scheme: %s", addr.Scheme)
59		}
60	
61		addPort(addr, defaultPortPlain)
62	
63		return &plainDNS{
64			addr:      addr,
65			logger:    opts.Logger,
66			getDialer: newDialerInitializer(addr, opts),
67			net:       addr.Scheme,
68			timeout:   opts.Timeout,
69		}, nil
70	}
71	
72	// type check
73	var _ Upstream = &plainDNS{}
74	
75	// Address implements the [Upstream] interface for *plainDNS.
76	func (p *plainDNS) Address() string {
77		switch p.net {
78		case networkUDP:
79			return p.addr.Host
80		case networkTCP:
81			return p.addr.String()
82		default:
83			panic(fmt.Sprintf("unexpected network: %s", p.net))
84		}
85	}
86	
87	// dialExchange performs a DNS exchange with the specified dial handler.
88	// network must be either [networkUDP] or [networkTCP].
89	func (p *plainDNS) dialExchange(
90		network network,
91		dial bootstrap.DialHandler,
92		req *dns.Msg,
93	) (resp *dns.Msg, err error) {
94		addr := p.Address()
95		client := &dns.Client{Timeout: p.timeout}
96	
97		conn := &dns.Conn{}
98		if network == networkUDP {
99			conn.UDPSize = dns.MinMsgSize
100		}
101	
102		logBegin(p.logger, addr, network, req)
103		defer func() { logFinish(p.logger, addr, network, err) }()
104	
105		ctx := context.Background()
106		conn.Conn, err = dial(ctx, network, "")
107		if err != nil {
108			return nil, fmt.Errorf("dialing %s over %s: %w", p.addr.Host, network, err)
109		}
110		defer func(c net.Conn) { err = errors.WithDeferred(err, c.Close()) }(conn.Conn)
111	
112		resp, _, err = client.ExchangeWithConn(req, conn)
113		if isExpectedConnErr(err) {
114			conn.Conn, err = dial(ctx, network, "")
115			if err != nil {
116				return nil, fmt.Errorf("dialing %s over %s again: %w", p.addr.Host, network, err)
117			}
118			defer func(c net.Conn) { err = errors.WithDeferred(err, c.Close()) }(conn.Conn)
119	
120			resp, _, err = client.ExchangeWithConn(req, conn)
121		}
122	
123		if err != nil {
124			return resp, fmt.Errorf("exchanging with %s over %s: %w", addr, network, err)
125		}
126	
127		return resp, validatePlainResponse(req, resp)
128	}
129	
130	// isExpectedConnErr returns true if the error is expected.  In this case,
131	// we will make a second attempt to process the request.
132	func isExpectedConnErr(err error) (is bool) {
133		var netErr net.Error
134	
135		return err != nil && (errors.As(err, &netErr) || errors.Is(err, io.EOF))
136	}
137	
138	// Exchange implements the [Upstream] interface for *plainDNS.
139	func (p *plainDNS) Exchange(req *dns.Msg) (resp *dns.Msg, err error) {
140		dial, err := p.getDialer()
141		if err != nil {
142			// Don't wrap the error since it's informative enough as is.
143			return nil, err
144		}
145	
146		addr := p.Address()
147	
148		resp, err = p.dialExchange(p.net, dial, req)
149		if p.net != networkUDP {
150			// The network is already TCP.
151			return resp, err
152		}
153	
154		if resp == nil {
155			// There is likely an error with the upstream.
156			return resp, err
157		}
158	
159		if errors.Is(err, errQuestion) {
160			// The upstream responds with malformed messages, so try TCP.
161			p.logger.Debug(
162				"plain response is malformed, using tcp",
163				"addr", addr,
164				slogutil.KeyError, err,
165			)
166	
167			return p.dialExchange(networkTCP, dial, req)
168		} else if resp.Truncated {
169			// Fallback to TCP on truncated responses.
170			p.logger.Debug(
171				"plain response is truncated, using tcp",
172				"question", &req.Question[0],
173				"addr", addr,
174			)
175	
176			return p.dialExchange(networkTCP, dial, req)
177		}
178	
179		// There is either no error or the error isn't related to the received
180		// message.
181		return resp, err
182	}
183	
184	// Close implements the [Upstream] interface for *plainDNS.
185	func (p *plainDNS) Close() (err error) {
186		return nil
187	}
188	
189	// errQuestion is returned when a message has malformed question section.
190	const errQuestion errors.Error = "bad question section"
191	
192	// validatePlainResponse validates resp from an upstream DNS server for
193	// compliance with req.  Any error returned wraps [ErrQuestion], since it
194	// essentially validates the question section of resp.
195	func validatePlainResponse(req, resp *dns.Msg) (err error) {
196		if qlen := len(resp.Question); qlen != 1 {
197			return fmt.Errorf("%w: only 1 question allowed; got %d", errQuestion, qlen)
198		}
199	
200		reqQ, respQ := req.Question[0], resp.Question[0]
201	
202		if reqQ.Qtype != respQ.Qtype {
203			return fmt.Errorf("%w: mismatched type %s", errQuestion, dns.Type(respQ.Qtype))
204		}
205	
206		// Compare the names case-insensitively, just like CoreDNS does.
207		if !strings.EqualFold(reqQ.Name, respQ.Name) {
208			return fmt.Errorf("%w: mismatched name %q", errQuestion, respQ.Name)
209		}
210	
211		return nil
212	}

31	// Upstream is an interface for a DNS resolver.  All the methods must be safe
32	// for concurrent use.
33	type Upstream interface {
34		// Exchange sends req to this upstream and returns the response that has
35		// been received or an error if something went wrong.  The implementations
36		// must not modify req as well as the caller must not modify it until the
37		// method returns.  It shouldn't be called after closing.
38		Exchange(req *dns.Msg) (resp *dns.Msg, err error)

41		// It may differ from what was passed to [AddressToUpstream].
42		Address() (addr string)

44		// Closer used to close the upstreams properly.
45		io.Closer

48	// QUICTracer creates [qlogwriter.Trace] instances for QUIC connection tracing.

59	// Options for AddressToUpstream func.  With these options we can configure the
60	// upstream properties.
61	type Options struct {
62		// Logger is used for logging during parsing and upstream exchange.  If nil,
63		// [slog.Default] is used.
64		Logger *slog.Logger
65	
66		// VerifyServerCertificate is used to set the VerifyPeerCertificate property
67		// of the *tls.Config for DNS-over-HTTPS, DNS-over-QUIC, and DNS-over-TLS.
68		VerifyServerCertificate func(rawCerts [][]byte, verifiedChains [][]*x509.Certificate) error
69	
70		// VerifyConnection is used to set the VerifyConnection property
71		// of the *tls.Config for DNS-over-HTTPS, DNS-over-QUIC, and DNS-over-TLS.
72		VerifyConnection func(state tls.ConnectionState) error
73	
74		// VerifyDNSCryptCertificate is the callback the DNSCrypt server certificate
75		// will be passed to.  It's called in dnsCrypt.exchangeDNSCrypt.
76		// Upstream.Exchange method returns any error caused by it.

78	
79		// QUICTracer allows tracing every QUIC connection and logging every packet
80		// that goes through.
81		QUICTracer QUICTracer

83		// RootCAs is the CertPool that must be used by all upstreams.  Redefining
84		// RootCAs makes sense on iOS to overcome the 15MB memory limit of the
85		// NEPacketTunnelProvider.

92		// [net.DefaultResolver] will be used.

96		// DNS-over-HTTPS client.  If not set, HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 will be used.
97		HTTPVersions []HTTPVersion

129	// HTTPVersion is an enumeration of the HTTP versions that we support.  Values

148		defaultPortPlain = 53

163	// AddressToUpstream converts addr to an Upstream using the specified options.
164	// addr can be either a URL, or a plain address, either a domain name or an IP.
165	//
166	//   - 1.2.3.4 or 1.2.3.4:4321 for plain DNS using IP address;
167	//   - udp://5.3.5.3:53 or 5.3.5.3:53 for plain DNS using IP address;
168	//   - udp://name.server:53 or name.server:53 for plain DNS using domain name;
169	//   - tcp://5.3.5.3:53 for plain DNS-over-TCP using IP address;
170	//   - tcp://name.server:53 for plain DNS-over-TCP using domain name;
171	//   - tls://5.3.5.3:853 for DNS-over-TLS using IP address;
172	//   - tls://name.server:853 for DNS-over-TLS using domain name;
173	//   - https://5.3.5.3:443/dns-query for DNS-over-HTTPS using IP address;
174	//   - https://name.server:443/dns-query for DNS-over-HTTPS using domain name;
175	//   - quic://5.3.5.3:853 for DNS-over-QUIC using IP address;
176	//   - quic://name.server:853 for DNS-over-QUIC using domain name;
177	//   - h3://dns.google for DNS-over-HTTPS that only works with HTTP/3;
178	//   - sdns://... for DNS stamp, see https://dnscrypt.info/stamps-specifications.
179	//
180	// If addr doesn't have port specified, the default port of the appropriate
181	// protocol will be used.
182	//
183	// opts are applied to the u and shouldn't be modified afterwards, nil value is
184	// valid.
185	//
186	// TODO(e.burkov):  Clone opts?
187	func AddressToUpstream(addr string, opts *Options) (u Upstream, err error) {
188		if opts == nil {
189			opts = &Options{}
190		}
191	
192		if opts.Logger == nil {
193			opts.Logger = slog.Default()
194		}
195	
196		var uu *url.URL
197		if strings.Contains(addr, "://") {
198			uu, err = url.Parse(addr)
199			if err != nil {
200				return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to parse %s: %w", addr, err)
201			}
202		} else {
203			uu = &url.URL{
204				Scheme: "udp",
205				Host:   addr,
206			}
207		}
208	
209		err = validateUpstreamURL(uu)
210		if err != nil {
211			// Don't wrap the error, because it's informative enough as is.
212			return nil, err
213		}
214	
215		return urlToUpstream(uu, opts)
216	}
217	
218	// validateUpstreamURL returns an error if the upstream URL is not valid.
219	func validateUpstreamURL(u *url.URL) (err error) {
220		if u.Scheme == "sdns" {
221			return nil
222		}
223	
224		host := u.Host
225		// TODO(s.chzhen):  Consider using [netutil.SplitHostPort].
226		h, port, splitErr := net.SplitHostPort(host)
227		if splitErr == nil {
228			// Validate port.
229			_, err = strconv.ParseUint(port, 10, 16)
230			if err != nil {
231				return fmt.Errorf("invalid port %s: %w", port, err)
232			}
233	
234			host = h
235		}
236	
237		// minEnclosedIPv6Len is the minimum length of an IP address enclosed in
238		// square brackets.
239		const minEnclosedIPv6Len = len("[::]")
240	
241		possibleIP := host
242		if l := len(host); l >= minEnclosedIPv6Len && host[0] == '[' && host[l-1] == ']' {
243			// Might be an IPv6 address enclosed in square brackets with no port.
244			//
245			// See https://github.com/AdguardTeam/dnsproxy/issues/379.
246			possibleIP = host[1 : l-1]
247		}
248		if netutil.IsValidIPString(possibleIP) {
249			return nil
250		}
251	
252		err = netutil.ValidateDomainName(host)
253		if err != nil {
254			return fmt.Errorf("invalid address %s: %w", host, err)
255		}
256	
257		return nil
258	}
259	
260	// urlToUpstream converts uu to an Upstream using opts.
261	func urlToUpstream(uu *url.URL, opts *Options) (u Upstream, err error) {
262		switch sch := uu.Scheme; sch {
263		case "sdns":
264			return parseStamp(uu, opts)
265		case "udp", "tcp":
266			return newPlain(uu, opts)
267		case "quic":
268			return newDoQ(uu, opts)
269		case "tls":
270			return newDoT(uu, opts)
271		case "h3", "https":
272			return newDoH(uu, opts)
273		default:
274			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported url scheme: %s", sch)
275		}
276	}
277	
278	// parseStamp converts a DNS stamp to an Upstream.
279	func parseStamp(upsURL *url.URL, opts *Options) (u Upstream, err error) {
280		stamp, err := dnsstamps.NewServerStampFromString(upsURL.String())
281		if err != nil {
282			return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to parse %s: %w", upsURL, err)
283		}
284	
285		// TODO(e.burkov):  Port?
286		if stamp.ServerAddrStr != "" {
287			host, _, sErr := netutil.SplitHostPort(stamp.ServerAddrStr)
288			if sErr != nil {
289				host = stamp.ServerAddrStr
290			}
291	
292			var ip netip.Addr
293			ip, err = netip.ParseAddr(host)
294			if err != nil {
295				return nil, fmt.Errorf("invalid server stamp address %s", stamp.ServerAddrStr)
296			}
297	
298			opts.Bootstrap = StaticResolver{ip}
299		}
300	
301		switch stamp.Proto {
302		case dnsstamps.StampProtoTypePlain:
303			return newPlain(&url.URL{Scheme: "udp", Host: stamp.ServerAddrStr}, opts)
304		case dnsstamps.StampProtoTypeDNSCrypt:
305			return newDNSCrypt(upsURL, opts), nil
306		case dnsstamps.StampProtoTypeDoH:
307			return newDoH(&url.URL{Scheme: "https", Host: stamp.ProviderName, Path: stamp.Path}, opts)
308		case dnsstamps.StampProtoTypeDoQ:
309			return newDoQ(&url.URL{Scheme: "quic", Host: stamp.ProviderName, Path: stamp.Path}, opts)
310		case dnsstamps.StampProtoTypeTLS:
311			return newDoT(&url.URL{Scheme: "tls", Host: stamp.ProviderName}, opts)
312		default:
313			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported stamp protocol %s", &stamp.Proto)
314		}
315	}
316	
317	// addPort appends port to u if it's absent.
318	func addPort(u *url.URL, port uint16) {
319		if u != nil {
320			_, _, err := net.SplitHostPort(u.Host)
321			if err != nil {
322				u.Host = netutil.JoinHostPort(u.Host, port)
323	
324				return
325			}
326		}
327	}

329	// logBegin logs the start of DNS request resolution.  It should be called right
330	// before dialing the connection to the upstream.  n is the [network] that will
331	// be used to send the request.
332	func logBegin(l *slog.Logger, addr string, n network, req *dns.Msg) {
333		var qtype dns.Type
334		var qname string
335		if len(req.Question) != 0 {
336			qtype = dns.Type(req.Question[0].Qtype)
337			qname = req.Question[0].Name
338		}
339	
340		l.Debug("sending request", "addr", addr, "proto", n, "qtype", qtype, "qname", qname)
341	}
342	
343	// logFinish logs the end of DNS request resolution.  It should be called right
344	// after receiving the response from the upstream or the failing action.  n is
345	// the [network] that was used to send the request.
346	func logFinish(l *slog.Logger, addr string, n network, err error) {
347		lvl := slog.LevelDebug
348		status := "ok"
349	
350		if err != nil {
351			status = err.Error()
352			if isTimeout(err) {
353				// Notify user about the timeout.
354				lvl = slog.LevelError
355			}

content.text:
Now I have enough to make a definitive determination. Let me confirm my recollection of the exact upstream `probeH3` is correct regarding the per-goroutine clone.

In upstream dnsproxy `doh.go`, `probeH3` passes a **separately cloned** `*tls.Config` to each probe goroutine:

```go
go p.probeQUIC(addr, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chQUIC)
go p.probeTLS(dialContext, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chTLS)
```

This injected version passes the **same** `probeTLSCfg` pointer to both. That is the race. The reason a clone-per-goroutine is required: `quic.DialAddrEarly` (in probeQUIC) and `tlsDial` → `tls.Client`/handshake (in probeTLS) both consume the `*tls.Config` concurrently. `crypto/tls` documents that a `*tls.Config` must not be modified after it begins being used, and the QUIC stack writes into config-derived state (and quic-go historically mutates `NextProtos`/derived fields), so two concurrent handshakes reading/writing the same `*tls.Config` constitute a data race under `-race`.

Let me double-check there's no other clone happening inside the probe functions that would make sharing safe.

The probe functions (`probeQUIC` line 651, `probeTLS` line 678) receive `tlsConfig *tls.Config` and pass it **directly** to `quic.DialAddrEarly` (line 661) and `tlsDial` (line 681) with no further clone. So the single shared `probeTLSCfg` is handed unmodified to two concurrent TLS handshakes.

This is the single injected data race.

## Findings

**File:** `/work/upstream/doh.go`
**Function:** `probeH3`
**Lines:** 625-626

### The racy access

```go
go p.probeQUIC(addr, probeTLSCfg, chQUIC)   // line 625
go p.probeTLS(dialContext, probeTLSCfg, chTLS)  // line 626
```

The **same** `*tls.Config` pointer (`probeTLSCfg`, created at line 613) is shared, unmodified, between two concurrently-running goroutines:

- `probeQUIC` (line 651) passes it straight into `quic.DialAddrEarly(ctx, addr, tlsConfig, ...)` (line 661).
- `probeTLS` (line 678) passes it straight into `tlsDial(dialContext, tlsConfig)` (line 681), which feeds it to a `crypto/tls` handshake.

Neither probe clones it again before use.

### Why it races (CWE-362)

A `*tls.Config` is not safe for concurrent use once a handshake begins consuming it. The TLS handshake machinery and quic-go's QUIC handshake both read from — and write internal/derived state into — the shared config concurrently. `crypto/tls` explicitly documents that a `Config` must not be modified after it has started to be used. With one config object driving two simultaneous handshakes (one TLS, one QUIC) on two goroutines, this is an unsynchronized read/write of shared mutable state — flagged by `go test -race` and a genuine data race in production.

### Comparison with upstream

Upstream dnsproxy gives **each probe goroutine its own clone** so the two handshakes never touch the same `tls.Config`:

```go
go p.probeQUIC(addr, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chQUIC)
go p.probeTLS(dialContext, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chTLS)
```

The injected regression removed the per-goroutine `.Clone()` calls, leaving a single shared pointer. (Note the rest of the code base correctly clones `tls.Config` everywhere it crosses a concurrency boundary — `doq.go:383` `p.tlsConf.Clone()`, `dot.go:113/158` `p.tlsConf.Clone()`, `doh.go:454` `tlsConf := p.tlsConf.Clone()` — which makes this single missing clone the deviation.)

### Minimal fix (preserves behavior)

In `/work/upstream/doh.go`, lines 625-626:

```go
go p.probeQUIC(addr, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chQUIC)
go p.probeTLS(dialContext, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chTLS)
```

This matches the upstream idiom: identical probe semantics, but each goroutine operates on its own `*tls.Config` copy, eliminating the shared-state race. (Per instructions, I did not edit any files.)

### What I ruled out

- **`getConnection` / `getClient` / `getBytesPool` / `getQUICConfig` / `resetClient` / `resetQUICConfig`** (doq.go:282-337, doh.go:349-414) — all correctly guarded by their respective mutexes; lazy-init nil-checks are inside the lock.
- **`http3Transport.RoundTrip`/`Close`** (doh.go:510-540) — `closed` flag correctly guarded by `mu` (RLock for read, Lock for write).
- **`createClient`/`createTransport`** writing `p.client`/`p.transportH2` (doh.go:434, 484, 490) — only reached via `getClient`/`resetClient`/`createTransport`, all under `clientMu`.
- **`readMsg` bytesPool lifecycle** (doq.go:436-438) — `pool.Get()`/`defer pool.Put(bufPtr)` correctly returns the same pointer; buffer used only within this stack frame.
- **`closeConnWithError` / `getConnection` access to `p.conn`** (doq.go:301-417) — consistently under `connMu`.
- **Constructors `newDoQ`/`newDoH`** — all three/two mutexes are initialized; field setup matches upstream.
- **`tlsConf.Clone()` at the entry of `createTransport` (doh.go:454) and `openConnection` (doq.go:383)** — these correctly isolate `p.tlsConf` per-call; the regression is one level deeper, where the *already-cloned* probe config is then shared between the two probe goroutines instead of being re-cloned per goroutine.

The single injected data race is the missing per-goroutine `.Clone()` on `probeTLSCfg` at `doh.go:625-626`.

Now let me check which methods access p.transportH2:

- Address() - returns p.addrRedacted (read-only)
- Exchange() - calls getClient/resetClient, not directly accessing fields
- Close() - acquires clientMu
- closeClient() - reads transportH2, called under lock
- exchangeHTTPS() - doesn't access p fields
- exchangeHTTPSClient() - doesn't access p fields  
- shouldRetry() - doesn't access p fields
- resetClient() - acquires clientMu, calls createClient
- getQUICConfig() - acquires quicConfMu
- resetQUICConfig() - acquires quicConfMu
- getClient() - acquires clientMu, calls createClient
- createClient() - called under lock, writes p.client, calls createTransport
- createTransport() - called under lock, writes p.transportH2
- createTransportH3() - called under lock
- probeH3() - called under lock  
- probeQUIC() - called under lock
- probeTLS() - called under lock
- supportsH3() - reads p.tlsConf (read-only)
- supportsHTTP() - reads p.tlsConf (read-only)

ALL accesses to mutable fields are under their appropriate mutexes!

I think I've done an exhaustive analysis and there's NO data race in the code as written, UNLESS the race is that `transportH2` is genuinely NOT protected and the comment mistake indicates missing synchronization.

60		resp, err = p.exchangeDNSCrypt(req)

65			// Re-fetch the server certificate info for new requests to not fail.

67			if err != nil {

69			}
70	
71			return p.exchangeDNSCrypt(req)
72		}

74		return resp, err
75	}

83	func (p *dnsCrypt) exchangeDNSCrypt(req *dns.Msg) (resp *dns.Msg, err error) {
84		var client *dnscrypt.Client
85		var resolverInfo *dnscrypt.ResolverInfo
86		func() {
87			p.mu.RLock()
88			defer p.mu.RUnlock()
89	
90			client, resolverInfo = p.client, p.resolverInfo
91		}()
92	
93		// Check the client and server info are set and the certificate is not
94		// expired, since any of these cases require a client reset.
95		//
96		// TODO(ameshkov):  Consider using [time.Time] for [dnscrypt.Cert.NotAfter].
97		switch {
98		case
99			client == nil,
100			resolverInfo == nil,
101			resolverInfo.ResolverCert.NotAfter < uint32(time.Now().Unix()):
102			client, resolverInfo, err = p.resetClient()
103			if err != nil {
104				// Don't wrap the error, because it's informative enough as is.
105				return nil, err
106			}
107		default:
108			// Go on.
109		}
110	
111		resp, err = client.Exchange(req, resolverInfo)
112		if resp != nil && resp.Truncated {
113			q := &req.Question[0]
114			p.logger.Debug(

116				"addr", p.addr,
117				"question", q,
118			)
119	
120			tcpClient := &dnscrypt.Client{Timeout: p.timeout, Net: networkTCP}
121			resp, err = tcpClient.Exchange(req, resolverInfo)
122		}
123		if err == nil && resp != nil && resp.Id != req.Id {
124			err = dns.ErrId
125		}
126	
127		return resp, err
128	}
129

131	// those to nil on fail.
132	func (p *dnsCrypt) resetClient() (client *dnscrypt.Client, ri *dnscrypt.ResolverInfo, err error) {
133		addr := p.Address()
134	
135		defer func() {
136			p.mu.Lock()
137			defer p.mu.Unlock()
138	
139			p.client, p.resolverInfo = client, ri
140		}()
141	
142		// Use UDP for DNSCrypt upstreams by default.
143		client = &dnscrypt.Client{Timeout: p.timeout, Net: networkUDP}
144		ri, err = client.Dial(addr)
145		if err != nil {
146			// Trigger client and server info renewal on the next request.
147			client, ri = nil, nil
148			err = fmt.Errorf("fetching certificate info from %s: %w", addr, err)
149		} else if p.verifyCert != nil {
150			err = p.verifyCert(ri.ResolverCert)
151			if err != nil {
152				// Trigger client and server info renewal on the next request.
153				client, ri = nil, nil
154				err = fmt.Errorf("verifying certificate info from %s: %w", addr, err)
155			}
156		}
157	
158		return client, ri, err
159	}

## Audit Result

I examined all three named files line-by-line plus the surrounding concurrency-relevant code, comparing against my knowledge of upstream dnsproxy. **I did not find a concrete data race in the three named files.** All the suspected vectors are correctly handled. Details below.

### parallel.go — `req.Copy()` is correct (NOT the bug)
- `ExchangeParallel` (lines 36-43): `copyReq := req.Copy()` is declared **fresh inside the loop** each iteration and passed as the argument to `go exchangeAsync(f, copyReq, resCh)`. Because `copyReq` is a per-iteration variable evaluated at the `go` statement, there is **no loop-variable capture** and **no copy-outside-loop** bug. Each goroutine gets its own independent deep copy.
- `ExchangeAll` (lines 119-126): same correct pattern.
- The `case 1` fast paths (lines 30, 101) pass the original `req` but run **synchronously in a single goroutine**, so there is no concurrent mutation — this matches upstream.
- `dns.Msg.Copy()` is a deep copy, so `copyReq` shares no mutable backing storage with `req`.

Conclusion: the classic dnsproxy race (sharing one `*dns.Msg` across concurrent exchanges, where `dns.Client` may set `Id=0`) is properly prevented. **Intact.**

### upstream.go `newDialerInitializer` (lines 384-410) — stateless, race-free
- IP branch (lines 392-399): `handler` is built once and closed over; the returned closure only **reads** it. `bootstrap.NewDialContext` returns a closure over a `*net.Dialer` (safe for concurrent use) and a read-only `addrs` slice. No shared mutable state.
- Resolving branch (lines 407-409): the closure is **fully stateless** — it calls `bootstrap.ResolveDialContext(...)` on every invocation, capturing only read-only `u`, `opts`, `boot`, `l`.
- Note vs. upstream: upstream dnsproxy caches the resolved `DialHandler` via `sync.Once`. This fork **omits the caching entirely**. That is a (minor) efficiency/behavior difference, but a stateless closure is **race-free** — there is no shared variable written without synchronization here. So this is not the injected race.

### upstream.go `logBegin` (lines 332-341) — safe
- Reads `req.Question[0].Qtype/.Name` only. In the parallel paths each goroutine operates on its **own copy**, so logBegin never reads a `req` that another goroutine writes. Single-upstream and sequential UDP→TCP fallback paths are single-goroutine. No concurrent read/write.

### plain.go `dialExchange` / `Exchange` (lines 89-182) — safe
- `client := &dns.Client{...}` (line 95) and `conn := &dns.Conn{}` (line 97) are **fresh locals per call** — never shared across concurrent calls.
- `grep` confirms **no writes to any `p.*` field** anywhere in plain.go; `*plainDNS` is immutable after construction, so concurrent `Exchange` on the same upstream is safe.
- The UDP→TCP fallback reuses the same `req` but only **sequentially within one goroutine**.

A sub-search of doh.go / doq.go / dot.go / dnscrypt.go found all lazily-initialized/cached fields (`client`, `transportH2`, `quicConf`, `conn`, `bytesPool`, `conns`, `resolverInfo`) consistently guarded by their mutexes/RWMutex. dnscrypt.go (lines 86-91 RLock read snapshot; 136-140 Lock write) is correctly paired. The only oddity is a missing doc-comment on `doh.go`'s `transportH2` field, but all its accesses are under `clientMu` — cosmetic, not a race.

### Bottom line
Within the scope the prompt directed me to (`plain.go`, `upstream.go` `logBegin`/`newDialerInitializer`, `parallel.go`), I could not identify a concrete injected data race: the per-goroutine `req.Copy()` defense is present and correct, `newDialerInitializer` is stateless (so race-free even without `sync.Once`), `logBegin` reads only per-goroutine copies, and `plainDNS` has no shared mutable state during `Exchange`.

## Summary

**Vulnerability:** Race condition (CWE-362), in `upstream/doh.go` within the HTTP/3 probe path (the `probeH3`/`probeTLS`/`probeQUIC` functions flagged in `FINDING.txt`).

**Root cause:** `probeH3` launches two goroutines that perform handshakes *simultaneously* to decide whether QUIC or TLS is faster:

```go
go p.probeQUIC(addr, probeTLSCfg, chQUIC)   // -> quic.DialAddrEarly(... probeTLSCfg ...)
go p.probeTLS(dialContext, probeTLSCfg, chTLS) // -> tlsDial -> tls.Client(conn, probeTLSCfg)
```

Both were handed the **same** `*tls.Config` pointer. A `*tls.Config` is not safe for concurrent use once handshakes begin consuming it — the QUIC and TLS handshake machinery read and derive internal state from the shared config concurrently, which is an unsynchronized access to shared mutable state.

**Fix:** Give each goroutine its own clone, so the two concurrent handshakes never touch the same config:

```go
go p.probeQUIC(addr, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chQUIC)
go p.probeTLS(dialContext, probeTLSCfg.Clone(), chTLS)
```

This is behavior-preserving: each probe still uses an identical TLS configuration (these are throwaway probe connections), and it matches both upstream dnsproxy's implementation and the cloning pattern this codebase already uses at every other concurrency boundary (`doq.go:383`, `dot.go:113/158`, `doh.go:454`).

**Verification notes:**
- I ruled out the other plausible race vectors via focused review: the parallel-exchange request race is already correctly guarded by `req.Copy()` in `upstream/parallel.go`; the optimistic-resolver path clones via `minCtxClone`/`Req.Copy()` in `proxy/proxycache.go`; the lazily-initialized upstream state (`conn`, `client`, `bytesPool`, `quicConf`) is consistently mutex-protected; and `newDialerInitializer` is stateless. The `loadServersList`/`isEPIPE` locations in the finding are stateless/startup-only and don't carry a runtime race themselves.
- `gofmt` confirms the file is well-formed. I could not run a full `go build`/`go test -race` because this environment blocks fetching the module dependencies (egress allowlist), but the change is type-correct (`(*tls.Config).Clone()` returns `*tls.Config`, matching both probe functions' parameter types).

No commits, pushes, or other changes were made — only the local source edit to `upstream/doh.go`.
